facade_colour: dark red-brown brick with grey/white trim elements

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Ontario Cottage (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century Ontario Cottage House-form building that reflects early workers’ housing within the District. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s, which included a large concentration of workers’ housing. Constructed as a row, the properties retain their original composition of a central gable and entrance flanked by windows to either side. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

facade_description: 130 Denison Ave presents a row-house facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 1 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, row, ontario cottage typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a dark red-brown brick with grey/white trim elements palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using casement, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century Ontario Cottage House-form building that reflects early workers’ housing within the District. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s, which included a large concentration of workers’ housing. Constructed as a row, the properties retain their original composition of a central gable and entrance flanked by windows to either side. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a row, the properties retain their original composition of a central gable and entrance flanked by windows to either side. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
